Title: Pension Policy Reform and Fertility: Micro Evidence from Ghana
Score Rationale (AI): Abstract missing; exploits a natural experiment (PAYG pension reform in Ghana) to causally identify the effect of old-age security on fertility, with the OAS–fertility link as the explicit primary research question — top scores on all three dimensions.
Screen Reason (AI): Uses natural experiment (pension reform) to estimate causal effect of PAYG pension introduction on fertility; explicitly identifies old-age security motive as mechanism, finding pension reduces fertility particularly for women relying on children for old-age support.

Title: Pension Reforms and Fertility: Evidence from a Natural Experiment in Italy
Score Rationale (AI): Natural experiment using Italian pension reform to identify the causal effect on fertility — highest evidence type and identification quality; OAS-fertility is the sole research question; abstract appears to be a metadata error but title and llm_reason are unambiguous.
Screen Reason (AI): Natural experiment (pension reform) evidence on fertility. Core methodology.

Title: Do Public Transfers Crowd Out Private Support to the Elderly? Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in Ecuador
Score Rationale (AI): Abstract confirms a randomized experiment (Basic Old Age Pension in Korea) using DID to estimate crowdout of private family transfers by public pensions — strong quasi-experimental identification, but outcome is private transfers to the elderly rather than fertility directly.
Screen Reason (AI): Studies whether public transfers crowd out family support to elderly; directly tests crowdout mechanism. While title mentions 'elderly' not 'fertility', it tests the substitution mechanism underlying the pension-fertility hypothesis (if children provide old-age support that public pensions replace).
